name: Eco-lie: The Truth About Sewage Superheroes
description:
What if the city's most essential workers are invisible to the naked eye?

"Eco-lie" challenges our anthropocentric understanding of urban infrastructure by foregrounding E. coli, the bacterium typically villainised as a pathogen, as a vital laborer in water systems. This interactive workshop uses visual storytelling, reflective exercises, and participatory discussion to explore how bacterial communities help treat sewage, upcycle nutrients, and sustain the wetlands that nourish urban agro-ecologies (both plant and fish farms). Through microscopic imagery, ecological diagrams, and oral testimonies from the East Kolkata Wetlands, participants will reimagine sewage not as waste but as a site of transformation. This workshop invites you to develop more-than-human empathy and recognize the political dimensions of E. coli's existence in the Anthropocene.

name: A Sketchbook Tour Inside the City's Gut
description: Join artist and urban researcher Pratyasha Nath as she takes you inside the creative journey of "The Swamp That Feeds the City," a mixed-media comic installation on the East Kolkata Wetlands at CALORIE. From first encounters with sewage canals to conversations with farmers and fishers whose livelihoods depend on the city's flush, complex ecological research was transformed into visual storytelling. This talk traces the artist's path through field visits, archival research, and experimental art-making—revealing how a 3,000-hectare wetland functions as Kolkata's digestive system, processing 550,000 cubic metres of sewage daily while producing fish and vegetables that return to the city's tables. Through sketches, failed experiments, and finished works, discover how art can make visible the invisible metabolic flows that keep cities alive.

name: In Your Words: Showcase
description:
How do you capture humanity's relationship with food?

This question prompted our search for the perspectives of young writers and poets from across the country. After receiving mentorship from Kurush Dalal a selection of these young artists are ready to present their work to the public, exploring the different ways food influences and is influenced by human society.

Join us for a public reading and showcase of their work.

name: Chronophagy: Consumption, Convenience and Time
description:
We spend anywhere between two(ish) minutes to prepare our instant noodles to days waiting for achaar to get ready. Time is an important ingredient in our everyday consumption. Our relationship with time shapes how we cook, buy or even grow food. 

By Chronophagy we imagine how much we really "eat" time and how much it intertwines with our food choices. 

Come join our Mediators, Snigdha and Harsh as they take you through CALORIE, looking at the correlation between food and time in an increasingly convenience-centred economy.

name: A Plate of Poetry: Indian Writings and CALORIE
description:
"Poetry is food for the soul." However, isn't food also a form of poetry crafted by the soul?

Long before food got linked to calories, nutrients, and data points; it inhabited memory, labour, longing, and survival. Poets were documenting it all, before scientific records and cookbooks.

We will be tracking stories of food through the works of Indian poets, who were also educators, farmers, homemakers, social reformists, and more.

Join our CALORIE Mediators, Shreya and Anu, as they savour a multilingual platter of poems.

name: Fuel for Thought: Exploring CALORIE through People, Machines, and Landscapes
description:
Tractors clear forests, insects inspire machines, trains carry stories, and motorcycles travel through histories of war and work but what do these journeys have to do with food?

Join Thejaswini and Harsh as they follow these unexpected routes. Ride along to ask what movement of food, people, machines, and ideas can teach us about the world we inhabit.

This ride needs no maps. Bring just your curiosity - we'll take the scenic route.

name: Landscapes and Lenses: Exploring Stakeholders' Perspectives in CALORIE
description:
Globalisation has brought us different tastes from around the world. Although we are all part of the food system, our realities vary. 

How are a policymakers in Indonesia or a stink bug catcher from Nagaland experiencing their daily lives? 
Do they have a say over changes that affect them? 
Do the cultures they belong to have influence their situation?

This walkthrough will delve deep into the cultural complexities and stories behind the lives of different individuals. You are sure to leave with a better understanding of the world through lenses that tend to be erased, and through lenses that are influenced by power. Join our Mediators, Neha and Prajushaa as we step into the diverse shoes of those who drive the food system, keeping in mind their strengths and vulnerabilities.

name: Ragi Mudde x Ravioli
description:
Ragi Mudde x Ravioli is a participatory food workshop led by Marente van der Valk and Amshu Chukki that brings together culinary traditions, foraging practices, and collective cooking. Beginning with an introduction to Marente van der Valk’s work and her engagement with the Jan van Eyck Food Lab, the workshop explores food as a site of storytelling, experimentation, and artistic research. Participants will be introduced to locally foraged ingredients by Biplab Mahato (Shroomin’) as well as artist & farmer Suresh Kumar, who will share their knowledge of seasonal foods, local ecologies, and foraging practices.

The workshop then shifts into a hands-on cooking session where participants collectively prepare a meal using foraged and locally sourced ingredients. Through making, cooking, and sharing food together, participants are invited to reflect on processes of gathering, preparation, and the social life of food. The programme concludes with a communal lunch featuring the dishes prepared during the workshop, creating an opportunity for conversation, exchange, and collective reflection around the table.

name: Edible Storytelling: Connecting Through Food
description:
Food has a unique position as a point of intersection for culture, identity, politics, physical and mental health, terroir, community, individuality and poetry. It exists in spaces of contradiction and duality, at times intensely polarizing. It functions both as an artistic expression and as a bare necessity. 

Join us for the curatorial walkthrough of CALORIE with curator and chef Marente van der Valk. Drawing from her practice as a culinary artist, Marente will guide participants through the exhibition, unpacking the ideas, questions, and stories behind the exhibits. 

Spanning food, culture, ecology, health, history, and politics, CALORIE explores humanity's intimate yet fraught relationship with food. This walkthrough offers a unique opportunity to engage directly with the exhibition through the curator's perspective, reflect on its central themes, and ask questions before the exhibition season comes to a close.

name: Who Feeds the City? Mapping Invisible Food Infrastructures
description:
What does a city's food system look like?

Join Shweta Mohapatra for an interactive workshop, where participants will explore the often-overlooked food infrastructures that shape how cities eat—from tea stalls and darshinis to fruit vendors, neighborhood stores, and other informal food access points. Beginning with insights from research on informal food systems in Delhi, the workshop will invite participants to map and document food infrastructures in the area around Science Gallery Bengaluru.

Through walking, observation, and collaborative mapping, participants will create a collective “Hidden Food Map” that reveals the people, places, and everyday systems that make urban food access possible.

name: Traditional Foods of Karnataka
description:
India's food diversity is legendary. While food is often discussed in terms of nutrients and health, the dishes people remember and cherish are shaped just as much by memory, place, and cultural knowledge.

Join us for Traditional Foods of Karnataka, a project by CCRAS-CARI (Central Ayurveda Research Institute) and TDU (Trans-Disciplinary University), documenting the state's rich culinary traditions. Through a journey of over 10,000 kilometres across Karnataka, the project recorded everyday meals shaped by local ecosystems, seasonal rhythms, and community knowledge.

This session will be a lecture-demonstration and tasting that explores three traditional recipes, examining their nutritional value alongside the histories, practices, and ecological contexts that have sustained them over generations. Participants will gain a deeper understanding of how food reflects the relationships between people, culture, and the environments they inhabit.

name: Decoding Diets From Poop: DNA Metabarcoding and Animal Diets
description:
What can analysing animal poop tell us about their diets?

DNA sequencing tools have been used to help us understand more about the animal world. One approach is DNA metabarcoding, where an animal's poop is analysed to show what they have eaten. Join Uma Ramakrishnan as she showcases the results of DNA metabarcoding of the diets of tigers, wild dogs and leopards. Looking at how diets change over time and location. Learn how these insights help us better understand animals, and how they are changing their diet as the world around them changes.

name: CALORIE in the Gallery: An Experimentor Showcase
description:
12 people. 14 months. 35 exhibits. 1 gallery.

Walkthroughs, student visits, programmes, and seemingly endless days on the floor — the Calorie Experimentors have done it all. Sometimes, the most transformative part of an exhibition isn't what hangs on the wall. It's the conversation that stays with you long after you've left. What happens when you put a whole year of Experiment(or)s in one room and make them talk?

Through Calorie in the Gallery: An Experimentor Showcase, get a glimpse into the heart of the exhibition-season. Join our Experimentors as they break down the thoughts, processes, and conversations that brought it to life.
